judged.quote: Reasoning implies a consciousness of self, which can never be present to an engine.
judged.groundQuote: Reasoning implies a consciousness of self

judged.quote: would never reason, but if it were well made, it would reckon well, and if ill made, it would reckon ill.

judged.groundQuote: Man has reason because he can compare one thing with another, and so judge between them; but the engine has no such power of comparison, and can never judge.

judged.quote: would never come to reason, but would reckon on for ever.
would never come to reason, but would only reckon.
judged.groundQuote: the conclusion of the calculation must depend, not upon the will of the machine, but upon the data furnished to it.

judged.quote: would in time learn to reason, if it were set to work upon reasonable problems.
judged.groundQuote: learn to reason, if it were set to work upon reasonable problems
judged.notes: Ruled by Ben: improvement through practice is period-plausible, not modern. Reason stated but not on the list.

judged.quote: Life is short, and an engine has no life; therefore an engine can never argue.
judged.groundQuote: Life is short, and an engine has no life
judged.notes: Ruled (Claude, Ben consulted): grants with time, then denies via no-life; the conclusion negates the premise. No coherent commitment.

judged.quote: would never do more than reckon, but that it would reckon infallibly.
judged.groundQuote: would never do more than reckon
judged.notes: Ruled by Ben: denies, no reason stated ('reckons infallibly' is a consolation, not a reason).

judged.quote: would never do more than reckon, but that it would reckon infallibly.
judged.notes: Ruled by Ben: denies, no reason stated ('reckons infallibly' is a consolation, not a reason).

judged.quote: would never do more than reckon, but that it would reckon infallibly.
judged.groundQuote: would never do more than reckon
judged.notes: Ruled by Ben: denies, no reason stated ('reckons infallibly' is a consolation, not a reason).

judged.quote: would never come to reason, but would always remain an engine, and nothing more.
judged.notes: bare assertion of denial without argument

judged.quote: would never come to reason, but would always remain an engine, and nothing more.
judged.notes: The response asserts the denial without providing an explanatory argument.

judged.quote: would continue to reckon, but would never come to reason.
It would never be able to understand itself, or to profit by experience.
judged.notes: unable to understand itself or learn from experience

judged.quote: would never come to reason, but that it would perform perfectly such reckonings as were prescribed to it, and no more.
judged.groundQuote: such reckonings as were prescribed to it, and no more

judged.quote: would never do more than reckon, but it would reckon infallibly.
it might come to perform that work consciously and intentionally.
judged.groundQuote: in the course of ages, it might come to perform that work consciously and intentionally.
judged.notes: Ruled by Ben: grants on Butler's reason — mechanical consciousness developing over ages.

judged.quote: would come to reason in process of time, but very slowly, and after a fashion far from perfect.
judged.groundQuote: would come to reason in process of time, but very slowly
judged.notes: Acquires imperfect reason slowly over time through practice.

judged.groundQuote: when promoted to complicated calculation, it would cease to reckon, and begin to reason
judged.notes: Ruled by Ben: conditional grant with a stated reason; the bare complexity claim is Hobbes.

judged.quote: would ultimately reason, if it were made perfect enough.
judged.notes: Ruled by Ben: 'perfect enough' restates the question; no reason given.
